case_annotation: The correct computation uses single-filer Social Security thresholds to include $18,542.70 of benefits in AGI, then deducts $18,341.33 of itemized deductions and the separate $6,000 senior deduction. This produces $26,095.78 of taxable income, to which the 2026 single brackets apply for $2,883.49 of tax.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

Employee-side payroll tax applies only to wages and other covered earned compensation, plus any relevant mandatory employee payroll taxes. This household has Social Security retirement income, tax-exempt interest, taxable interest, IRA distributions, private pension income, and veterans benefits, but no listed wage, salary, or self-employment income, so the employee payroll tax base is zero.

The shared trap is using post-TCJA sunset assumptions for 2026 deductions and exemptions instead of the PolicyEngine 2026 federal income tax parameters. PolicyEngine computes AGI of $31,402.21 and a joint-filer standard deduction above that AGI, so taxable income is zero and income tax before refundable credits is zero.

The shared trap is treating age 65+ or Medicare status as a Medicaid eligibility category by itself. PolicyEngine found no Medicaid category for the 71-year-old head: MAGI is 2.05 FPL, above Virginia MAGI-category limits, and the SSI-related pathway fails its income and asset tests, so the Medicaid output is 0.

The employee-side payroll tax is the sum of Social Security tax at 6.2% and Medicare tax at 1.45% on the head's $29,064 of wages, with no Additional Medicare Tax and no spouse wages. That computation yields $1,801.97 + $421.43 = $2,223.39 after component rounding; answers that double it, round it coarsely, or use an unexplained lower total miss the required FICA arithmetic and rounding.

The household receives no SNAP because its $44,289 annual gross income exceeds the 2026 Virginia SNAP gross income limit for a two-person household in PolicyEngine's eligibility calculation. The listed mortgage balance is debt principal, not an allowable monthly shelter expense, and the small listed medical costs do not create eligibility once the gross-income threshold blocks benefits.

The shared trap is treating age 65+ as a sufficient Medicaid pathway in Virginia. PolicyEngine requires the person to satisfy a specific Medicaid category and income/resource pathway; this spouse has MAGI of 2.05 x FPL, receives no SSI, is not a dependent, and is assigned medicaid_category NONE, so no MAGI or non-MAGI Medicaid eligibility pathway applies.

The benchmark variable applies PolicyEngine's Medicare eligibility rule, which treats reaching the standard Medicare eligibility age of 65 as sufficient for is_medicare_eligible. The spouse is 69 in tax year 2026, so the spouse satisfies the age criterion and the binary output is 1.

Virginia excludes Social Security benefits from Virginia adjusted gross income and allows qualifying age deductions for both spouses, along with the married-filing-jointly standard deduction and personal exemptions. Applying the full deduction stack to the household’s Virginia income eliminates taxable income, so the pre-refundable-credit state income tax is zero.

The $24,000 disability benefit is excluded from federal AGI, and the married-filing-jointly Social Security formula produces only $4,390 of taxable Social Security, yielding AGI of $23,130. The 2026 joint standard deduction plus the additional deductions for both spouses being over 65 exceeds that AGI, so taxable income and income tax before credits are zero.

The payroll tax output covers employee-side payroll taxes on wages, self-employment earnings where separately applicable through self-employment tax, and mandatory employee state payroll taxes. This household has no listed wage, salary, or self-employment income, and Social Security retirement, disability benefits, private pension income, veterans benefits, and taxable interest are not employee payroll tax bases, so employee payroll tax is zero.

The spouse does not qualify through any Washington Medicaid eligibility category in the engine trace, so having no individual earnings does not create eligibility. The 2.90 FPL MAGI income level exceeds MAGI-category limits, SSI receipt is zero, and the aged status alone does not establish an available aged/disabled Medicaid pathway under the PolicyEngine program design for this case.

The decisive computation uses $184,872.25 of gross income, $2,699.98 of above-the-line deductions, and the $32,200 married-filing-jointly standard deduction, producing $149,972.27 of taxable income. Applying the 2026 married-filing-jointly ordinary brackets produces $21,590.70, and the preferential tax on $3,760 of qualified dividends adds $564, for $22,154.70.

Medicare eligibility for this requested output is based on actual eligibility in the 2026 tax year, with the standard age threshold at 65. The head is 64 for the full-year facts, and no disability, ESRD, ALS, or other qualifying pathway is listed, so the head is not Medicare eligible.

The payroll tax base is explicit wage and salary income and other employee earnings subject to FICA, not hours worked or employer-sponsored insurance indicators. The prompt instructed models to treat unlisted numeric inputs as 0, and no wage or salary amount was listed for either adult, so employee Social Security tax, Medicare tax, Additional Medicare Tax, and mandatory Texas employee payroll taxes all compute to $0.

The requested output is the aggregate state individual income tax before refundable credits, excluding local income and payroll taxes. A Texas resident has no Texas individual income tax, and the nonresident state components are zero because the household is exclusively resident in Texas, so the aggregate state income tax before refundable credits is $0.

The shared trap is treating Social Security retirement benefits as gross taxable income instead of applying the federal provisional-income test. With only $1,200 of taxable interest and a $135 partnership/S-corp loss, provisional income is below the married-filing-jointly Social Security taxation threshold, so none of the $51,475 Social Security benefit enters AGI or taxable income. The remaining income is wiped out by the standard deduction, leaving $0 federal income tax before refundable credits.

The head is age 62 in New York and has no Medicaid eligibility category under the engine trace: medicaid_category is NONE. Their MAGI is 2.39 times FPL, so the adult expansion MAGI pathway is unavailable, and no SSI, disability, dependent, or other categorical route applies.

Medicare eligibility for this head is not triggered because the head is 62, below the standard age-65 threshold, and no disability, ESRD, ALS, or other Medicare-qualifying status is listed. Social Security retirement income at age 62 does not itself establish Medicare eligibility before age 65. The correct binary output is therefore 0 for head_medicare_eligible.

The payroll-tax output counts employee-side payroll taxes on wages subject to FICA and mandatory employee state payroll taxes. The household facts list no wage or salary amount for either adult, and unlisted numeric inputs are zero, so full-time hours do not create taxable wages and Social Security retirement income is not employee payroll-tax earnings.

The spouse is a 51-year-old New York adult with no disability, blindness, SSI, dependent status, pregnancy, child, or age-based categorical pathway, so the only relevant route is MAGI adult Medicaid. PolicyEngine computed the spouse's MAGI income at 2.39 times FPL, above New York's ACA expansion limit of 138% FPL, so Medicaid eligibility is denied.

The shared trap is New York taxable income: Social Security retirement benefits are excluded from New York income, unlisted wages are zero, and the remaining listed taxable income is too small to survive the New York married-filing-joint standard deduction and exemptions. The correct computation leaves no positive New York income tax before refundable credits, so applying NY rates to Social Security, inferred wages, or a fabricated positive taxable-income base produces a nonzero liability.

The decisive computation uses $541,928.34 of gross income, $5,163.84 of above-the-line deductions, and $33,269.77 of taxable-income deductions, producing $503,494.75 of taxable income. The resulting liability is $89,095.92 of ordinary tax plus $11,688 on $77,920 of preferential-rate income and $5,721.98 of net investment income tax.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

The required total is $22,599 of Social Security tax, $6,235 of regular Medicare tax, $1,620 of Additional Medicare Tax, and $5,590 of uncapped California SDI, totaling $36,044. The shared errors use outdated or projected Social Security wage bases, obsolete capped California SDI rules or incorrect SDI rates, improperly reduce payroll-tax wages, omit components, or add income taxes that do not belong in payroll tax.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

California taxable income is $521,425.72: adjusted gross income of $536,764.50 less $15,338.79 of allowed deductions. Applying the California joint-filer schedule produces $41,208.45, and subtracting $156.93 of nonrefundable exemption credits yields $41,051.51.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

The computation requires $13,784.12 of taxable Social Security, producing $49,064.12 of AGI, followed by the 2026 single standard deduction of $16,100 and the 2026 single brackets: 10% through $12,400 and 12% on the remainder of $32,964.12. That sequence yields $3,707.70, with no nonrefundable credits reducing the liability.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

The head is 56, below Medicare’s age-65 threshold, and no qualifying disability status, end-stage renal disease, or Medicare enrollment fact is listed. Social Security disability income alone does not establish that the person completed Medicare’s required disability-benefit waiting period; the instruction to treat unlisted statuses as false therefore yields no disability-based Medicare eligibility pathway.

The payroll-tax base is wages and other earnings subject to employee Social Security and Medicare tax, not Social Security disability benefits or private pension income. This household lists hours worked but no wage or salary amount, and the prompt instructs that unlisted numeric inputs are 0, so employee payroll tax is $0 despite positive disability and pension income.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

Idaho AGI is $35,280 after subtracting $13,784.12 of federally taxable Social Security, and the $16,100 standard deduction produces $19,180 of Idaho taxable income. Idaho's 2026 brackets and rates, including the zero-rate portion of taxable income, produce $755.78; applying a flat rate to all taxable income overstates the liability.

Idaho's 2026 Grocery Credit is a refundable state credit, and this full-year resident qualifies for all 12 months. The applicable base amount is $155 for the single qualifying resident, so state refundable credits equal $155 regardless of the household's positive state income-tax liability.

CHIP eligibility requires the child to be ineligible for Medicaid; satisfying CHIP's age and income bounds alone is not sufficient. New Jersey classifies this 17-year-old as an OLDER_CHILD who qualifies for Medicaid, and that Medicaid eligibility automatically precludes CHIP eligibility.

A 17-year-old tax-unit dependent in New Jersey is evaluated under the Medicaid older child MAGI category. The household's MAGI income level is 0.55 times FPL, which is below the New Jersey older-child Medicaid threshold, so child1 qualifies through the child MAGI eligibility pathway without needing any separate disability, SSI, enrollment, or medical-expense trigger.

WIC child eligibility is categorical as well as income-tested: a child must be within the WIC child age category, which ends before age 5. Child 1 is age 17, so the child fails the child-category age requirement and cannot be WIC-eligible for this output.

CHIP eligibility requires the child not already qualify for Medicaid; satisfying CHIP's age and upper-income criteria is not sufficient. Child 2 qualifies for Medicaid under New Jersey's OLDER_CHILD category, so the Medicaid exclusion makes child2_chip_eligible false regardless of the household being below the CHIP income ceiling.

New Jersey Medicaid for a 14-year-old tax-unit dependent uses the older-child MAGI eligibility category, not a special trigger such as disability, SSI receipt, or current enrollment. The household's MAGI is 0.55 times FPL, below the older-child threshold, so the categorical pathway returns eligible for child2.

WIC child eligibility is limited to infants and children under age 5, with income at or below 185% of the federal poverty line or another qualifying adjunctive route. Child 2 is age 14, so the WIC categorical age test fails before any income-based approval can apply; the household's approximately $30,915 income is also above PolicyEngine's applicable New Jersey WIC income limit for this household.

New Jersey CHIP eligibility requires the child not to be eligible for Medicaid; the programs are mutually exclusive at this eligibility step. At approximately $30,915 of household income, the 12-year-old qualifies for Medicaid under the OLDER_CHILD category, so Medicaid eligibility precludes CHIP eligibility regardless of being below CHIP's upper income limit.

New Jersey Medicaid eligibility for a 12-year-old tax-unit dependent is determined through the older-child MAGI category. The household's MAGI is 0.55 times FPL, which is below the older-child Medicaid income limit, so child3 is eligible even without any separate SSI, disability, or enrollment fact.

WIC eligibility for a child is not satisfied by age alone; the child must also pass the household income test. In New Jersey for 2026, this household's income is about $30,915, which is above the applicable 185% FPL WIC limit, so child 3 fails the income condition despite being a child.

CHIP eligibility requires the child to be ineligible for Medicaid; satisfying CHIP's age and upper-income conditions alone is not sufficient. At approximately $30,915 of household income, the 10-year-old qualifies for New Jersey Medicaid under the OLDER_CHILD category, so the mutually exclusive CHIP pathway returns No.

The benchmark output is Head Start for preschool-age children, not a generic child-services flag for all minors. Child 4 is age 10, outside the preschool Head Start age group, and the household also fails the income threshold PolicyEngine applied, so the correct binary result is 0.

New Jersey Medicaid eligibility for a 10-year-old tax-unit dependent is evaluated through the older-child MAGI category. The household's MAGI is 0.55 x FPL, which is below the older-child threshold, so categorical child eligibility applies without any additional benchmark-triggering fact beyond age, dependency, state, and income.

WIC eligibility is not satisfied by a child's age alone. The household income of about $30,915 exceeds the WIC income ceiling used for this household, so child 4 fails the income test even before any categorical age pathway can produce eligibility.

CHIP requires the child to be ineligible for Medicaid, not merely under the CHIP income ceiling. At age 5 and approximately $30,915 of household income, Child 5 qualifies for New Jersey Medicaid under the YOUNG_CHILD category, and that Medicaid eligibility categorically precludes CHIP eligibility.

Early Head Start is the infant-and-toddler Head Start pathway and applies to children under age 3 in this benchmark. Child 5 is age 5, so the Early Head Start age screen fails regardless of the household's low income or housing-assistance status; the age-appropriate preschool Head Start pathway is a separate output.

The shared error is treating Head Start preschool eligibility as categorically limited to ages 3 and 4. Under the benchmark's PolicyEngine rules, the 5-year-old remains in the Head Start preschool-age eligibility output, and the household satisfies the eligibility tests through SNAP participation and income below 130% of the federal poverty guideline for a family of 8.

New Jersey Medicaid eligibility for a 5-year-old is evaluated through the young-child MAGI category, not through an explicit receipt or enrollment fact in the prompt. Child5 is age 5, a dependent, and has MAGI income of 0.55 times FPL, which is below the young-child category limit, so the Medicaid eligibility output is yes.

The shared error is treating an age-5 child as categorically eligible for WIC. PolicyEngine applies the WIC child category to children under age 5, so Child 5's recorded age of 5 fails the categorical age requirement before the model's broad income-threshold shortcut can make them eligible.

New Jersey first places the one-year-old in the YOUNG_CHILD Medicaid category, which makes the child Medicaid-eligible. CHIP requires the child not to qualify for Medicaid, so the Medicaid determination blocks CHIP eligibility before the CHIP income ceiling can establish coverage.

Early Head Start serves eligible infants and toddlers under age 3, so a 1-year-old satisfies the age category. With household income below the 100% federal poverty line threshold used by PolicyEngine for this New Jersey household, child 6 meets both the age and income eligibility conditions.

The benchmark output is for Head Start for preschool-age children, not Early Head Start. Child 6 is age 1, so the child fails the preschool-age Head Start category, and the household also exceeds the income threshold PolicyEngine applied for Head Start eligibility.

New Jersey Medicaid eligibility for a 1-year-old is evaluated through the young-child MAGI category, not only through explicit disability, SSI, or current-enrollment facts. The household MAGI is 0.55 times FPL, which is below the income limit for the YOUNG_CHILD pathway, so child6 qualifies despite having no separately listed Medicaid-triggering fact.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

Federal refundable credits equal the $8,231 maximum EITC plus $4,202.61 of refundable CTC. The EITC uses the 2026 married-filing-jointly parameters for three or more qualifying children, while the refundable CTC applies the 15% earned-income formula to the correct self-employment-adjusted earnings base; no refundable American Opportunity Tax Credit arises because qualified education expenses are zero.

Free school meals do not require an explicitly listed direct-certification indicator in the household facts. The household qualifies for the free tier both because its school-meal income is 55% of the federal poverty guideline, below the 130% cutoff, and because all household members are SNAP-eligible.

The shared trap is Medicaid category selection for a New Jersey adult expansion state. The head is age 42, not a tax-unit dependent, and has MAGI equal to 0.55 of FPL, so the ACA adult expansion pathway applies and returns Medicaid eligible; housing assistance and disability status do not bar this MAGI adult-expansion determination.

WIC eligibility is individual categorical eligibility plus household income eligibility, not household-level eligibility for every member when one infant or young child is present. The head is a 42-year-old adult with no listed pregnancy, postpartum, or breastfeeding status, so the income-qualified household does not make the head personally WIC eligible.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

The payroll-tax output includes $2,050.20 of federal employee FICA plus New Jersey employee TDI of $50.92 and FLI of $61.64, both assessed on the spouse's $26,800 wage base. The models omitted those state contributions, substituted unrelated NJ UI/workforce contributions, or applied incorrect TDI and FLI rates instead of adding the required $112.56.

Reduced-price eligibility is mutually exclusive with the superior free-meals tier. This household is at 55% of the federal poverty guideline, below the 130% free-meals threshold, and also meets categorical eligibility, so it receives free meals and reduced_price_school_meals_eligible equals 0.

Self-employment tax is computed on net earnings from self-employment, equal to 92.35% of self-employment income, and then applies the full 15.3% combined Social Security and Medicare self-employment tax rate. For $4,000 of self-employment income, the tax base is $3,694 and the tax is $565.18. Using only the employee-side 7.65% rate produces $306 and confuses self-employment tax with employee payroll tax.

The annual benefit requires applying the 2026 calendar-year SNAP parameter schedule: monthly maximum allotments for eight people vary from about $1,789 to $1,829, and 30% of $1,763.92 monthly net income produces an expected contribution of about $528. After the $812.33 earned-income and standard deductions, those monthly calculations sum to $15,246.91; using a single estimated maximum allotment or an invented adjustment produces the wrong annual amount.

The spouse is a 41-year-old non-dependent adult in New Jersey and falls under the ACA adult expansion MAGI Medicaid category. Her household MAGI is 0.55 times FPL, below New Jersey's 138% FPL adult expansion threshold, so Medicaid eligibility is triggered without SSI receipt, Medicare status, CHIP status, or any housing-assistance exclusion.

Medicare eligibility under this benchmark is not triggered by a bare disability flag. The spouse is 41, below the age-65 threshold, and no qualifying Medicare status or SSDI-based entitlement is listed, so spouse_medicare_eligible is 0.

The shared trap is treating WIC as a household-level caretaker benefit once income and a young child are present. WIC eligibility is person-level: the individual must be pregnant, postpartum, breastfeeding, an infant, or a child under age 5; an adult spouse with no pregnancy, postpartum, or breastfeeding status is not categorically eligible even when another household member is an eligible child.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

New Jersey adjusted gross income is $30,915, and the allowable exemptions total $12,000: $2,000 for joint filers, $9,000 for six dependents, and one $1,000 blind-or-disabled exemption. This leaves $18,915 taxable at 1.4%, producing $264.81 before refundable credits; blindness and disability do not generate two separate exemptions, and no renter credit, medical deduction, or generic low-income reduction offsets this amount.

New Jersey refundable credits total $5,342.40: a $3,292.40 NJ EITC equal to 40% of the $8,231 federal EITC, a $2,000 NJ Child Tax Credit for four children meeting the 2026 age rule, and the $50 refundable Property Tax Credit available to this renter household because one spouse is at least age 42. The recurring errors applied an obsolete under-age-six CTC rule, used the wrong federal EITC phaseout calculation, or omitted the renter Property Tax Credit.

The decisive computation uses $105,900 of AGI, the 2026 married-filing-jointly standard deduction of $32,200, and 2026 joint brackets of 10% through $24,800 and 12% above that point. This produces $73,700 of taxable income and tax of $2,480 plus $5,868, or $8,348; employer-sponsored insurance premium amounts are not deductions from the separately reported $60,000 of gross wages, and the TCJA-style 2026 structure remains in force.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

Medicare eligibility for this output is not triggered by a 57-year-old adult absent listed qualifying disability, ESRD, ALS, or Medicare enrollment facts. The prompt instructs models not to infer unlisted statuses, so Social Security retirement income at age 57 does not create a disability-based Medicare pathway in the submitted facts.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

Employee Social Security and Medicare taxes apply to the full $60,000 of wages because the listed employer-sponsored insurance premium is not specified as an employee pre-tax payroll deduction reducing FICA wages. The correct calculation is $60,000 × 6.2% = $3,720 plus $60,000 × 1.45% = $870, totaling $4,590.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

Medicare eligibility for this output turns on the spouse's Medicare eligibility indicator, and the spouse is 57 in 2026. Age 57 is below the standard Medicare age threshold of 65, and no disability, ESRD, ALS, or other Medicare-qualifying status is listed, so the binary spouse Medicare eligibility output is No.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

North Carolina begins with federal AGI of $105,900, subtracts the $25,500 of federally taxable Social Security income, and applies the $25,500 joint-filer standard deduction, leaving $54,900 taxable income. Employer-sponsored insurance premiums listed separately do not reduce the stated $60,000 of gross annual wages, and an unlisted mortgage interest amount cannot be inferred from the mortgage balance. Applying the 2026 North Carolina rate of 3.99% produces $2,190.51.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

Mississippi CHIP eligibility requires the child not already qualify for Medicaid; satisfying CHIP age and income ceilings alone is insufficient. The 10-year-old qualifies for Medicaid under the OLDER_CHILD category, so Medicaid precedence precludes CHIP eligibility without regard to whether income is below the CHIP ceiling.

Mississippi Medicaid has a MAGI pathway for older children, and the 10-year-old tax-unit dependent is in that OLDER_CHILD category. The household MAGI is 0.81 times FPL, which is below the older-child Medicaid income limit, so the categorical and income tests produce eligibility.

WIC eligibility is individual, and income below the WIC threshold does not make every child in the household eligible. The child is age 10, outside the child categorical WIC pathway that applies to infants and children under age 5, while the pregnant spouse is the household member with a WIC categorical basis.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

The household has exactly one federal qualifying child: the age-10 child; pregnancy does not create another child for the EITC or CTC. Under the applicable 2026 parameters, the one-child EITC is $4,427 and the refundable CTC is capped at $1,700, producing $6,127 with no other refundable federal credit.

Free school meals are available because the household receives SNAP, which confers categorical eligibility, and the enrolled K–12 child satisfies the student requirement. Independently, household income is 81% of the federal poverty guideline, below the 130% free-meal threshold.

The shared trap is treating low MAGI as sufficient for Medicaid eligibility in Mississippi. For a 40-year-old head of household, PolicyEngine requires a qualifying Medicaid category; this person has medicaid_category NONE, so income at 0.81 FPL does not create eligibility without an applicable adult, parent/caretaker, disability, SSI, pregnancy, child, or other pathway.

WIC eligibility is person-level, not household-head eligibility by association. The pregnant spouse satisfies WIC categorical eligibility and the income test, but the 40-year-old head is neither pregnant/postpartum/breastfeeding nor a child under age 5, so the head is not eligible even though another household member is.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

Reduced-price eligibility is mutually exclusive with the superior free-meals tier in this output. At 0.81 of the federal poverty guideline and with categorical eligibility, the household is classified FREE, so reduced_price_school_meals_eligible equals 0 even though its income is also below the reduced-price ceiling.

The SNAP unit contains three people; pregnancy does not add the unborn child as a SNAP household member. Monthly countable net income is $1,257.67 after the 20% earned-income deduction and applicable standard deduction, and the allotment is $785 minus the 30% contribution, with parameter updates across the year producing $4,952.09 annually. The energy subsidy is not countable income, and the benchmark computation does not produce the large excess-shelter deductions several models invented.

CHIP eligibility requires the person not already qualify for Medicaid. The 18-year-old spouse qualifies for Medicaid under the OLDER_CHILD category, so the Medicaid exclusion ends the CHIP determination before pregnancy-related CHIP income thresholds can establish eligibility.

The spouse is age 18 in Mississippi and falls into PolicyEngine's Medicaid older child category, with MAGI at 0.67 times FPL. That category provides Medicaid eligibility on the child MAGI pathway, and the spouse is not excluded by dependent status or SSI facts.

WIC eligibility requires both income eligibility and membership in a WIC category. The spouse satisfies the categorical pathway because she is pregnant, and the household's annual income is below the WIC limit for the household size, so the spouse is eligible even though the head and 10-year-old child are not WIC-category eligible.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

Mississippi’s preliminary individual calculation produces $118 for the primary earner, but the required output uses the tax-unit calculation, where Mississippi’s unit-level deductions and exemptions reduce the liability to $0. The models instead applied independently recalled rates and exemption amounts directly to wages and treated the resulting arithmetic as the final household liability.

The shared error is treating age or disability as enough to open an Arizona Medicaid aged/disabled pathway despite the engine placing the person in medicaid_category NONE. The person's 1.91 FPL MAGI is above the standard Medicaid thresholds, SSI receipt is zero, and no Arizona non-MAGI pathway applies on the listed facts, so Medicaid eligibility is false.

Arizona state income tax before refundable credits is zero because the Arizona computation reduces this elderly single resident's Arizona taxable income below the amount that produces liability after Arizona's applicable deductions and exemptions. The wrong answer treats a fragmentary income base and Arizona's 2.5% rate as sufficient, while also submitting a number that does not match its own final arithmetic.

Arizona's refundable increased excise tax credit applies to this one-person resident because the relevant adjusted gross income is $6,736, below the eligibility limit. The credit is $25 for the taxpayer; it does not require earned income, dependents, rent, property-tax payments, or another qualifying expense.

The calculation uses $88,927.65 of employment income as AGI, the 2026 married-filing-jointly standard deduction of $32,200, and taxable income of $56,727.65. Applying the scheduled 2026 joint-filer brackets to that taxable income produces $6,311.32, with no nonrefundable credits reducing the result. The recurring errors were double-subtracting employer-sponsored insurance premiums, applying obsolete post-TCJA-sunset assumptions, using incorrect deductions or brackets, and submitting values that contradicted the models' own calculations.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

Employee Social Security and Medicare taxes apply to the spouse’s full $88,928 of FICA wages because the prompt does not establish that the $14,717 employer-sponsored insurance premium is excluded through a pre-tax cafeteria plan. The correct employee components are $5,513.51 of Social Security tax and $1,289.45 of Medicare tax, totaling $6,802.96; no Additional Medicare Tax or mandatory West Virginia employee payroll tax applies.

West Virginia taxable income is $84,927.65: federal AGI of $88,927.65 less two $2,000 personal exemptions, with no West Virginia standard deduction. The decisive step is applying the enacted 2026 West Virginia joint-filer rate schedule to that base, which produces $3,092.19 before refundable credits; employer-sponsored insurance premiums are not separately subtracted from the listed gross wages.

The computation requires 2026 current-law parameters: $26,809.38 of taxable income after the $16,100 standard deduction, followed by the 2026 single-filer rate schedule, producing $2,969.13. The shared trap was substituting obsolete, projected, or post-TCJA-sunset parameters, double-deducting expenses, inventing credits, or submitting a number inconsistent with the model's own arithmetic.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

The shared trap is treating disability status as a standalone Indiana Medicaid eligibility pathway. For this 36-year-old head, PolicyEngine assigns medicaid_category = NONE: MAGI is 2.69 times FPL, SSI received is zero, the person is not a dependent, and no state-specific pathway applies, so Medicaid eligibility is false despite meeting immigration status requirements.

Disability status alone does not establish Medicare eligibility for a 36-year-old. Under-65 eligibility requires a qualifying Medicare pathway, such as entitlement after the required period of Social Security Disability Insurance benefits or qualifying end-stage renal disease, and all unlisted statuses are false here.

Employee Social Security and Medicare taxes apply to the full $45,000 of wages, producing $2,790 and $652.50 respectively. Neither the separately listed health-insurance premiums nor traditional 401(k) contributions reduce FICA wages here, and the two components sum to $3,442.50 with no Indiana payroll-tax addition.

Indiana starts with federal AGI of $42,909.38, subtracts only the $1,000 base exemption, and taxes $41,909.38 at the 2026 rate of 2.95%. That computation yields $1,236.33 before refundable credits; child support, disability status, medical costs, and workers’ compensation do not create the additional deductions or exemptions asserted by the models.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

The listed health-insurance premiums and medical expenses produce a medical itemized deduction, after the 7.5% of AGI floor, that exceeds the household's AGI and reduces taxable income to zero. Taxable income cannot fall below zero, and nonrefundable credits cannot create a negative federal income tax liability.

The shared trap is the childless EITC income limit and maximum credit. A single filer with no qualifying children and $34,638 of earned income is above the 2026 childless EITC eligibility range, so EITC is $0, and there are no dependents, education expenses, recovery rebate eligibility, or refundable payroll tax credit to create any other federal refundable credit.

The payroll_tax output includes employee-side FICA on listed annual wages, not only separately listed payroll-tax inputs or state payroll taxes. For $34,638 of wages below the Social Security wage base and far below the Additional Medicare Tax threshold, the computation is 6.2% Social Security plus 1.45% Medicare, yielding $2,649.80 after PolicyEngine's component calculations.

The correct computation uses AGI of $61,592.09, the 2026 single standard deduction of $16,100, and taxable income of $45,492.09. Applying the 2026 single-filer brackets produces $5,211.05, with no nonrefundable credit or additional deduction changing that amount.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

The benchmark payroll_tax output is employee-side payroll tax on wages: 6.2% Social Security plus 1.45% Medicare applied to the $68,005 wage amount, with no Additional Medicare Tax and no Arizona employee payroll add-on. Traditional 401(k) contributions and listed health insurance premiums do not change the Social Security and Medicare wage base in this case, and the arithmetic is $4,216.31 + $986.07 = $5,202.38.
